coredata.dc:description: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing systems have surged in popularity in recent years, making significant strides in sensing and biosensing applications. The realization of high-throughput ECL sensors hinges on the implementation of novel signal amplification strategies, propelling the field toward a new era of ultrasensitive analysis. A key strategy for developing advanced ECL sensors and biosensors involves utilizing novel structures with remarkable properties. The past few years have witnessed the emergence of MXenes as a captivating class of 2D materials, with their unique properties leading to exploitation in diverse applications. This review provides a comprehensive summary of the latest advancements in MXene-modified materials specifically engineered for ECL sensing and biosensing applications. We thoroughly analyze the structure, surface functionalization, and intrinsic properties of MXenes that render them exceptionally suitable candidates for the development of highly sensitive ECL sensors and biosensors. Furthermore, this study explores the broad spectrum of applications of MXenes in ECL sensing, detailing their multifaceted roles in enhancing the performance and sensitivity of ECL (bio)sensors. By providing a comprehensive overview, this review is expected to promote progress in related areas.

head.ce:title: Emerging trends and recent advances in MXene/MXene-based nanocomposites toward electrochemiluminescence sensing and biosensing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing systems have surged in popularity in recent years, making significant strides in sensing and biosensing applications. The realization of high-throughput ECL sensors hinges on the implementation of novel signal amplification strategies, propelling the field toward a new era of ultrasensitive analysis. A key strategy for developing advanced ECL sensors and biosensors involves utilizing novel structures with remarkable properties. The past few years have witnessed the emergence of MXenes as a captivating class of 2D materials, with their unique properties leading to exploitation in diverse applications. This review provides a comprehensive summary of the latest advancements in MXene-modified materials specifically engineered for ECL sensing and biosensing applications. We thoroughly analyze the structure, surface functionalization, and intrinsic properties of MXenes that render them exceptionally suitable candidates for the development of highly sensitive ECL sensors and biosensors. Furthermore, this study explores the broad spectrum of applications of MXenes in ECL sensing, detailing their multifaceted roles in enhancing the performance and sensitivity of ECL (bio)sensors. By providing a comprehensive overview, this review is expected to promote progress in related areas.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sensors and biosensors have garnered significant attention as potential substitutes for the intricate, costly, and cumbersome traditional analytical equipment utilized in healthcare technology (Chamorro-Garcia and Merkoçi, 2016; Parolo and Merkoçi, 2013; Perfézou et al., 2012; Quesada-González and Merkoçi, 2015, 2017; Turner, 2013). Across decades, numerous devices have been crafted to monitor essential analytes through optical, electrochemical, electrochemiluminescence (ECL), and piezoelectric transducers (Bonaldo et al., 2023; Hu et al., 2022). Among them, ECL sensors have found a substantial position in the monitoring of targets. Ever since Kuwana, Hercules, Bard, and others initiated the first studies on ECL in the mid-1960s, continuous researches on it have been persisting so that in the modern world, ECL (bio)sensors have become an extremely potent analytical tool (Hercules, 1964; Kuwana et al., 1963; Santhanam and Bard, 1965). ECL refers to light emission by excited luminophores due to electrochemical processes taking place at the surface of an electrode. Benefiting from unique strengths such as the absence of a necessity for external light sources, minimal background interference, precise control over the spatiotemporal characteristics of light and remarkable sensitivity, ECL has emerged as a prominent signal transduction method applied extensively across various fields. (Arab et al., 2024; Firoozbakhtian et al. 2022, 2023b; Ma et al., 2019; Miao, 2008). The design and development of sensors and biosensors to identify chemical and biochemical analytes stands as a significant research area in this regard (Babamiri et al., 2019). Within the realm of ECL (bio)sensor development, paramount significance is placed upon the reliable detection of desired analytes (Sobhanie et al., 2022). Consequently, paramount emphasis during the design of ECL (bio)sensors is placed on achieving exceptional selectivity and sensitivity (Sobhanie et al., 2023).

With the advent of nanomaterials, sensitivity and diversity of ECL biosensors has been profoundly transformed. A diverse range of nanostructured materials, varying in sizes, shapes, chemical compositions, and distinctive properties ranging from metal oxide nanoparticles, carbon-based nanomaterials, and metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have been exploited for construction of ECL (bio)sensors to attain high sensitivity and selectivity. (Adhikari et al., 2020; Du et al., 2022a; Guo et al., 2011; Hosseini et al., 2017; Pur et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2020). Nevertheless, the pursuit of engineering nanomaterials boasting superior traits and outstanding analytical performance, resulting in the advancement of ultra-sensitive ECL (bio)sensors is still going on.

MXenes, a recently emerged class of two-dimensional (2D) transition metal carbides, nitrides, or carbonitrides, have attracted considerable interest owing to their remarkable potential across diverse applications (Huang et al., 2020; Murali et al., 2022). They can be categorized into mono-transition-metal (mono-M) MXenes and double transition-metal according to their transition-metal composition. Mono-transition metal MXenes, which have been more extensively studied than their counterparts, often represented by the formula Mn+1XnTx (n = 1, 2, 3). In this context, M denotes a transition metal (e.g., Ti, Sr, V, Cr, Nb), X refers to carbon or nitride, and T representing the associated functionality (Damirchi et al., 2024; Shahzad et al., 2022; Thomas and Cherusseri, 2023; Zahedi et al., 2024). MXenes are conveniently synthesized via the selective etching of the “A” element from its MAX phase precursor. MAX phases represent a general class of ternary layered compounds, where the “A” element typically is an element from groups 13 to 16 of the periodic table. (Anasori et al., 2017; Arshian et al., 2024; Kazerouni et al., 2024). Upon selective removal of the A-group layers, the transition metals in Mn+1Xn are exposed. Subsequently, the M-elements become terminated with surface functional groups, resulting in the chemical formula Mn+1XnTx. Etching and post-treatment methods, as well as the inherent properties of the transition metal, affect the composition of surface terminations on MXenes (Kazerouni et al., 2024; Li et al., 2020; Zahedi et al., 2024).

Because of their unique properties, MXenes have the potential to revolutionize sensing applications (Bhardwaj et al., 2022; Kalambate et al., 2019; Karadurmus et al., 2023; Tian et al., 2023; Tran et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023). They possess excellent electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic property, high electron transfer capability, creditable enzyme loading capacity, hydrophilicity, abundant surface functional groups, tunable surface chemistry, easy surface functionalization and modification (Otgonbayar and Oh, 2023). The slim nanosheets of 2D layered MXenes have a large surface area and surface functional groups. This characteristic enables tailored modifications targeted at biomolecules and conductivity adjustments, rendering them remarkably adaptable for the detection of diverse biomolecules. The incorporation of MXene with other (nano)materials frequently results in improved performance compared to each component individually, benefiting the distinctive properties of MXene in conjunction with the specific attributes of the integrated nanomaterials (Riazi et al., 2021; Solangi et al., 2023). MXene-based nanocomposites hold promise for a wide range of applications across fields, especially sensing and biosensing (Li and Cheng, 2022; Yang et al., 2023). In 2018, MXene was first introduced into the ECL sensing field, resulting in higher detection sensitivity in the ECL sensing platforms (Fang et al., 2018). Subsequently, a range of ECL sensors based on MXenes/MXene-based (nano)composite were developed for bioanalysis.

In this review we provide a targeted overview of recent research concerning ECL sensors and biosensors utilizing MXenes. Initially, we investigate the synthesis methods and properties of MXene to offer readers a comprehensive understanding. Then, we delve into their potential roles in development of ECL (bio)sensor. Finally, we discuss the major challenges and prospects for future MXene-based materials in ECL sensors and biosensors. We anticipate that this review offers insights into the ongoing research directions and methodologies using MXene nanocomposites to develop efficient ECL sensors and biosensors with high selectivity and sensitivity. Scheme 1 illustrates a doughnut chart showcasing the most important synthesis ways, prominent properties, and the utilization of MXenes in developing ECL (bio)sensors. It is worth saying that in the latter, the categorization is centered on the key role of MXene in establishing ECL (bio)sensors. To our knowledge, this is the first comprehensive review on MXenes/MXene-based nanocomposite for ECL sensing and biosensing, and we hope it will benefit the readers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and surface functionalization of Mxenes
ce:section.ce:para: The efficacy of any material is contingent upon its synthesis methodology, as the internal geometry of the material is intricately linked to the synthesis techniques employed. To date, over 30 distinct MXenes have been synthesized through various methodologies, like electrochemical synthesis (Sun et al., 2017b), wet etching methods including hydrofluoric acid (HF) etching (Li et al., 2018) and molten salt etching (Urbankowski et al., 2016), atomic layer deposition (Zhang et al., 2021b), microwave-assisted synthesis (Wu et al., 2021), urea glass route (Ma et al., 2015a), ultrasonication (Malaki et al., 2019), and chemical vapor deposition (CVD) (Wang et al., 2023a). Among these procedures, wet etching techniques have emerged as the dominant approach for the synthesis of these structures.

HF etching reigns supreme as the most widely used technique for MXene synthesis. It leverages the unique reactivity of HF towards the A-group element within the layered MAX phase precursor. During etching, the HF selectively dissolves the A-group element. This targeted attack

Progressively weakens the interlayer bonding within the MAX phase, eventually leading to the delamination of the transition metal carbide/nitride layers. These negatively charged transition metal carbide/nitride sheets, rich in surface terminations like -OH and -F due to the etching process, constitute the final MXene product (Malaki et al., 2019). Gogotsi et al. were the first to utilize this method to synthesize Ti3C2Tx, employing concentrated HF acid (50 wt%) at room temperature (Naguib et al., 2011). The resulting product exhibited an accordion-like structure composed of a collection of weakly stacked 2D sheets.

While HF etching offers effectiveness in MXene synthesis, its application is accompanied by a substantial safety concern due to its hazardous nature. The highly corrosive properties of HF necessitate stringent safety protocols and specialized handling procedures to minimize risks during synthesis. This has spurred the exploration of alternative etching methods like molten salt etching to in-situ HF-forming.

This method relies on the reaction between a weaker acid (usually HCl) and fluoride-based salts (LiF, CaF, NaF, KF, etc.) to generate HF in situ within the etching solution. As a result, the concentration of HF typically ranges between 5 and 10%, unlike the commercially available 50 wt% HF (Alhabeb et al., 2017). This method might require longer etching times or higher temperatures compared to direct HF etching, and the choice of fluoride source can influence the surface terminations on the final MXene products. Currently, these two methods have emerged as the primary approach for MXene preparation due to its straightforward operation and high yield.

The surface groups present on the outer layers of MXene offer numerous possibilities for functionalization through covalent or non-covalent bonding (Boota et al., 2020). Because of the existence of -OH terminal groups, MXene can be functionalized using silylation reagents to produce MXene nanosheets patterned with amino silane moieties. This modification enables NHS/EDC-based amine coupling, allowing for the covalent immobilization of receptors, such as antibodies (Kumar et al., 2018). MXenes can also be modified with zwitterionic molecules like sulfobetaine and carboxybetaine to develop superior anti-fouling surfaces. This modification significantly reduces non-specific protein binding compared to unmodified MXenes.

Furthermore, the inherent negative surface charge of MXenes allows for functionalization via electrostatic interaction. Positively charged molecules can electrostatically bind to MXene, offering a simple and versatile strategy (Sun et al., 2017a).

The electrochemical and electronic characteristics of MXenes rely entirely on factors such as structure, composition, surface chemistry, size of the flakes, and interlayer spacing. The majority of MXenes exhibit metallic electron transport behavior attributed to the presence of free electrons on transition metals serving as carriers (Xu et al., 2021). However, MXenes offer electronic tunability, a feature distinct from other metals, owing to surface terminations that can modulate the density of states (DOS) and Fermi level (Hart et al., 2019). The surface terminations of MXenes can influence the work function, due to alterations in the induced dipole moment. The OH termination predominantly induces a negative dipole moment on the surface, whereas the O termination results in a positive surface dipole moment. Fluorine, on the other hand, can induce either a negative or positive dipole moment. The presence of surface dipole moments or differing electronegativities among various functional groups presents an opportunity to functionalize or immobilize specific biomarkers, such as antigens, antibodies, or particular nanomaterials, thereby enhancing the sensitivity of the transducing interface (Kumar et al., 2018).

The optical characteristics of MXenes are entirely contingent upon the structure, M and X sites, and stoichiometry of surface terminal groups. MXenes display transverse and longitudinal surface plasmon modes within the visible and near-infrared regions (Gonçalves et al., 2021). Due to inter-band transitions, a pronounced absorption band is also detected in the UV range (Lim et al., 2021). The absorption of light in the UV and NIR ranges arises from plasmonic effects and electronic transitions (Chen et al., 2021a). The plasmonic characteristics exhibited by MXenes have paved the way for sensing applications and spurred the advancement of MXene-based plasmonic-enhanced photodetectors.

Carbon and nitrogen establish stable and robust bonds with metals, resulting in MXenes possessing exceptional mechanical properties. The mechanical characteristics of MXenes are intricately determined by a combination of factors including the elements M-X, surface terminal groups, defect density, size, incomplete nanosheet edges, and overall structure (Zhang et al., 2020a). However, as with other 2D materials, the fundamental mechanical properties are mostly governed by the individual flakes (Shekhirev et al., 2021). Despite difficult techniques to evaluate MXenes' mechanical properties, it is anticipated that nitride MXenes exhibit a higher Young's modulus compared to carbide MXenes (Zou et al., 2021).

MXenes indicate commendable chemical stability across diverse environments, with their layered structure shielding the metal core from oxidation. Stability is contingent upon several factors. For example, different types of MXene exhibit differing degrees of stability, influenced by the metal's susceptibility to oxidation. -F group also offers superior stability against harsh chemicals compared to -OH groups. Nevertheless, employing certain strategies can enhance stability. Surface functionalization and the introduction of stable functional groups can passivate the surface, fortifying resistance to oxidation and degradation (Palisaitis et al., 2018).

The dispersibility of MXene in polar solvents, facilitated by their surface functionalities, is notable. Charged surface groups induce electrostatic repulsion between MXene sheets, impeding aggregation. Techniques like sonication further improve dispersion by overcoming weak van der Waals forces between sheets (Maleski et al., 2017). Factors such as surface terminations, pH, and ionic strength play significant roles in affecting dispersibility.

MXenes are inherently hydrophilic due to surface terminations like hydroxyl (-OH) and fluorine (-F) groups. The inherent hydrophilicity of MXenes is often associated with biocompatibility, making them promising candidates for robust, label-free biomolecule detection (Fan et al., 2020). Additionally, their hydrophilic nature and large surface area enable MXenes to effectively load other nanomaterials, drugs, as well as ionic and polar species (Lu et al., 2021). Nevertheless, crafting biocompatible and flexible active films remains a challenging endeavor and is a subject of ongoing research. While some studies report the cytotoxicity of MXenes, others showcase their potential for various biomedical applications (Wang et al., 2020). However, the limited number of articles address cytocompatibility in vitro/vivo. Various parameters, including surface properties, size, composition, and surface modification, play crucial roles in influencing biocompatibility and cytocompatibility (Rozmysłowska-Wojciechowska et al., 2020). Predicting the structure/property correlation of MXene with cytocompatibility remains challenging, necessitating further studies to be conducted in this area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Significance of MXene in ECL biosensor development
ce:section.ce:para:
MXenes/MXene-based nanocomposites have emerged as a prominent class of materials in the development of ECL sensing and biosensing platforms. Their exceptional properties render them well-suited for ECL sensing and biosensing applications.
⁃
Support for immobilization of biorecognition elements and luminophores: MXene's large surface area and versatile surface chemistry make it an ideal platform for immobilization of biorecognition elements and/or luminophores (Bjork and Rosen, 2021; Kumar et al., 2023; Li et al., 2023a; Persson and Rosen, 2019).
⁃
Facilitated interfacial electron transfer: The inherent conductivity of MXene allows it to function as an electron bridge, promoting efficient electron transfer between the electrode and other ECL reaction components. This minimizes interfacial charge transfer resistance and further facilitates the electron transfer process for enhancing ECL signals (Bhardwaj et al., 2022). Furthermore, MXene's surface functional groups can be tailored to create favorable electrostatic interactions with luminophores, further accelerating electron transfer at the crucial electrode-luminophore interface (Ding et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2021).
⁃
Co-reactant accelerator effect: MXene's role in ECL sensors extends beyond simply facilitating electron transfer. It can participate in reactions with the co-reactant s, facilitating the decomposition of them and leading to the generation of more active intermediates to enhance ECL signals (Yin et al., 2023).
⁃
Strong reduction ability of MXene: some types of MXene like Ti3C2 are highly sought-after for constructing MXene/metal nanoparticle hybrids, due to their strong reduction ability. This reduction ability facilitates in situ formation of metal nanoparticles on the MXene surface during synthesis, eliminating the requirement for a reducing agent. The unique properties of MXenes enable the fabrication of surfactant-free nanohybrids, making them particularly attractive for biosensing applications. Furthermore, they could enable the in situ formation of some metal nanoclusters as emitters with excellent performance for biosensing applications (Zhang et al. 2016b, 2020b).
⁃
Tunable faraday cage effect: In specific designs, MXene's conductive nature can be harnessed to create a localized Faraday cage effect. This shielding effect protects the ECL reaction from external electromagnetic interference, ensuring high signal fidelity and minimizing background noise. The ability to tune the electromagnetic shielding properties of MXene through controlled synthesis and surface modification opens exciting avenues for the development of highly reliable and robust ECL sensors (He et al., 2023; Verma et al., 2023).
⁃
Precursor to synthesize luminescent nanomaterial: Recent research indicates that MXenes offer a promising avenue for synthesizing various luminescent materials. This includes the development of MXene-derived emitters with enhanced luminescent properties, opening up new possibilities for advanced sensing, imaging, and optoelectronic applications (Sharbirin et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2017).
MXene's multifaceted functionalities offer a treasure trove of opportunities for advancing ECL sensor technology. In the subsequent section, we delve into each of these attributes in detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recent advances in MXene/MXene-based nanocomposites for ECL sensor and biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: MXenes have been extensively utilized in developing biosensors for monitoring proteins, nucleic acid, exosomes, etc. In this section, we focus on the application of MXene in developing sensors and biosensors. In addition to providing insights into the fabrication process and operational mechanism, a comprehensive examination of the role played by MXene in the reviewed articles is also conducted. Table 1 summarizes the relevant parameters and merits of the developed sensors.

The remarkable surface area of MXene emerges as one of the most prominent features of MXene, rendering it an exceptional candidate to the development of ECL sensors. This expansive surface area provides abundant binding sites for immobilizing different biorecognition elements, including nucleic acids, antibodies, enzymes, etc. This facilitates the development of diverse biosensors with enhanced performance characteristics (Shahzad et al., 2022).

For example, in 2022, Wu et al. developed a biosensor for monitoring SARS-CoV-2 using polymetallic particles and Ti3C2@Ag-Au nanocomposite, HP-assisted target cycling, and dumbbell hybridization chain amplification (DHCA) techniques (Fig. 1 A). They embedded Ag-Au nanoparticles within Ti3C2 as an electrochemical interfacial material, which was subsequently deposited on the GCE's surface. Employing the stem-loop structure of hairpin (HP) DNA and leveraging the cleavage property of exonuclease III (Exo III), the target input was converted into essential ssDNA output, to establish the three-way junction structure necessary for stacked DHCA. After the amplification process, a substantial quantity of Ru (phen)3 2+ was captured by insertion into the dsDNA grooves, and the resulting signal was ultimately output by the ECL photoelectric system. They found that Ti3C2@Ag-Au significantly increased nucleic acid capacity and electron transfer efficiency at the electrochemical interface, improving the efficiency and intensity of ECL. (Wu et al., 2022). In particular, MXenes/MXene-based nanocomposites have been found widespread applications in constructing immunosensors. It can provide a favorable platform for the immobilization of both capture and detection antibodies, a crucial step in facilitating specific antigen-antibody interactions. For instance, in 2023, Firoozbakhtian et al. presented a single-electrode electrochemical system for point-of-care testing of SARS-CoV-2 utilizing a carbon ink modified with Ti3C2 (Fig. 1B). They mixed carbon ink with an adequate amount of Ti3C2, applying a silk stencil for screen printing, and used it to immobilize the biorecognition component. By connecting their platform to a Raspberry Pi 4 B computer, which served as both the power source for generating the ECL and the computational power required for sample analysis, they were able to detect SARSCoV-2 in 18 individual reaction cells, allowing for simultaneous analysis while maintaining sample isolation (Firoozbakhtian et al., 2023a). In 2020, hang et al. synthesized Ti3C2 -Au NPs hybrids to immobilize detection antibodies. They designed a dual-potential ratiometric ECL strategy for detecting carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) using luminol as the sole luminophore. Luminol demonstrated cathodic emissions on graphene-ionic liquid-platinum (GR-IL-Pt) composites and anodic emissions on Ti3C2 -Au NPs hybrids. Next, they constructed a sandwich ECL sensor, employing GR-IL-Pt composites as a matrix to immobilize the primary antibodies of CEA and Ti3C2-Au NPs hybrids as a platform to load the secondary antibodies. In the presence of CEA, there was a noticeable increase in the ratio of anodic ECL to cathodic ECL, enabling sensitive ratiometric detection of CEA. Moreover, the ratio ECLanodic/ECLcathodic remained independent of the concentrations of H2O2, significantly enhancing the reliability of the test (Shang et al., 2020).

As previously stated, MXenes are distinguished by their extraordinary reduction ability, making them highly sought after for the synthesis of MXene/metal nanoparticle hybrids. For instance, MXenes/Ag nanocomposites and MXenes/magnetic iron oxide nanocomposites have been synthesized utilizing MXenes' strong reducing abilities (Zhang et al., 2016a; Zhang et al., 2016b; Zou et al., 2016). These composites exhibit exceptional electrocatalytic activity and diverse catalytic abilities, highlighting the potential of MXenes in biosensing applications. Leveraging MXene's ability to reduce metal ions without requiring stabilizers or reducing agents, researchers have successfully synthesized innovative nanocomposites.

For example, in 2020, Zhang et al. devised an ECL biosensor for the detection of exosomes and their surface proteins by creating a hybrid of in situ-formed AuNPs decorated on Ti3C2, modified with aptamers. In their approach, the exosomes were captured on an electrode interface modified with CD63 aptamers, which specifically recognize exosomes. Simultaneously, the in-situ formation of gold nanoparticles occurred on single-layer Ti3C2 modified with aptamers (Ti3C2-Apt). MXenes served dual roles as both reductants and stabilizers, eliminating the need for additional reductants and stabilizers. According to their findings, the in-situ formation of AuNPs-Ti3C2-Apt hybrids not only demonstrated highly specific recognition of exosomes but also offered a bare catalytic surface with high electrocatalytic activity due to the predominance of facets in the gold nanoparticles. This enhancement significantly improved the ECL signal of luminol (Zhang et al., 2020b).

The remarkable surface area, outstanding electrical conductivity, minimized carrier diffusion length, and adaptable surface chemistry of MXene position it as a promising candidate for immobilizing luminophores. This capability holds the potential to enhance ECL signals, ensuring amplified and resilient detection outcomes (Kalambate et al., 2019).

In 2022, Wang et al. fabricated an ECL biosensor for PNK detection based on the immobilization of Ru (bpy)3 2+ on Ti3C2TX. Initially, they utilized Ti3C2TX nanosheets as effective substrates to enable the in-situ generation of AuNPs anchored on Ti3C2TX to form Ti3C2TX-AuNPs via the reducibility of Ti. The reduction of Au salt facilitated by the surface reducibility of Ti resulted in the synthesis of AuNPs directly anchored on Ti3C2TX. Subsequently, they immobilized Ru (bpy)3 2+ onto the Ti3C2TX-AuNPs through electrostatic interactions, wherein the positively-charged luminophore interacts with the negatively-charged Ti3C2TX nanomaterial, yielding Ru-Ti3C2TX-AuNPs. Finally, they recognized DNA phosphorylated with PNK by the chelation between Ti and phosphate groups (Wang et al., 2022a).

MXenes can serve as an excellent substrate for incorporating various organic ECL emitters like porphyrins or organometallic complexes. These emitters face limitations in ECL sensors due to their inadequate electrical conductivity, as well as poor solubility and dispersion in aqueous solutions. Porphyrins feature large heterocycles and extensive π-conjugated systems, rendering them susceptible to electron transfer reactions and showcasing high efficiency in separating electron-hole pairs (Liu et al., 2019). However, porphyrins are prone to aggregation due to unfavorable π-π stacking interactions, particularly in aqueous environments and subsequently reducing luminescent efficiency. To tackle the challenge of porphyrin aggregation, some potential strategies involve integrating them with other materials or immobilizing them through MOFs to augment the capabilities of porphyrin molecules (Sun et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2023).

For the development of porphyrin-based nanocomposites, MXene stands out as the first candidate. The high electronegativity of the terminal atoms or functional groups on the MXene surface makes them powerful electron acceptors and facilitates the deprotonation of porphyrin ligands to bridge with the metal atoms in MXene. Moreover, only atomic-layer-thick 2D MXenes possess a substantial accessible surface, offering numerous sites for ligand attack. Leveraging the unique properties of MXene nanosheets enables the coordinated coupling of porphyrin molecules, thereby establishing favorable conditions for the fabrication of porphyrin/MXene nanocomposites (Wu et al., 2019). In newly conducted research by Sun et al. a dual-signal response system involving FL “signal-on” and ECL “signal-off” has been designed for the detection of alkaline phosphatase. They synthesized a nanocomposite exhibiting phosphate-induced stimuli-responsive behavior, comprised of zinc (II) meso-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin and Ti3C2Tx (ZnTCPP/Ti3C2Tx) hybrids. This was achieved through the coordination binding between the carboxylated zinc (II) porphyrin ligands and the Ti sites present on the surface of Ti3C2Tx. According to their findings, the inclusion of MXene facilitated electron transfer, improving the ability of surface ZnTCPP to activate O2 and generate singlet oxygen (1O2), thereby enhancing cathodic ECL. Moreover, PO4 3− interacted with the Ti sites of ZnTCPP/Ti3C2Tx, competing for coordination with ZnTCPP. This competition led to the detachment of ZnTCPP, resulting in increased FL and decreased ECL (Sun et al., 2024). In another research, Xie et al. constructed a biosensor by applying [Ir (pbi)2 (acac)]@Ti3C2Tx-polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) hydrogel composite as an ECL emitter for determination of dopamin based on efficiently quenching of ECL signal. Through the use of Ti3C2Tx, they achieved the stabilization of Ir (pbi)2 (acac), thereby amplifying the ECL signal of the system. Following a detailed investigation of the electrochemical and co-reactant ECL mechanisms, they figured out that dopamin can efficiently quench the ECL of the Ir@MXene-PVA/DBAE system (Xie et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MXene as biorecognition element and luminophore carrier
ce:section.ce:para: Through an extensive examination of the literature, it becomes evident that the predominant application of MXene/MXene-based nanocomposites involves its dual role as both a biorecognition element and a luminophore carrier in biosensing systems. Exploiting the extraordinary characteristics of MXene, researchers have utilized this material as an optimal platform for the immobilization of both luminophores and biorecognition molecules, enabling the detection and quantification of a diverse range of targets. For example, in 2020 fang et al., developed an ECL and photothermal dual-mode biosensor for exosome detection (Fig. 1C). They initially modified a GCE with SiO2 nanourchin (SiO2 NUs) to serve as a sensing platform for immobilizing aptamers. Then, they synthesized a heterostructure consisting of black phosphorous quantum dots (BPQDs) and Ti3C2 to serve as a support, aiming to increase the immobilization of Ru (dcbpy)3 2+ and antibody, further enhancing the ECL signal. The exosomes immobilized on the electrode could detect the signal probe labeled with CD63 antibody, enabling dual signal readout in both ECL and temperature for exosome determination (Fang et al., 2020b). In 2021, Yao et al. designed an ECL-RET strategy for detecting the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) gene of SARS-CoV-2 (Fig. 1D). At first, they fabricated an Au@Ti3C2@PEI-Ru (dcbpy)3 2+ nanocomposite to modify GCE, serving as a base for immobilizing hairpin DNA and a swing arm-blocker (signal on). Then, they utilized multiple hairpin DNAs (HP DNAs) excised by the unipedal DNA walker's amplification strategy, powered by Nb.BbvCI endonuclease energy. Ultimately, they applied model DNA−Ag nanoclusters (model DNA−AgNCs) to quench the ECL signal. Ti3C2 not only provided good electrical conductivity but also had a large specific surface area, which could uniformly disperse Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) and load a large amount of Ru- (dcbpy)3 2+ (Yao et al., 2021). In 2022, Jiang et al. developed an aptasensor by synthesizing Ti3C2 decorated with well-dispersed AgBr nanocrystals (NCs) (Ti3C2–AgBrNCs) for the determination of enrofloxacin as a model analyte. They observed that the Ti3C2–AgBrNCs nanocomposites exhibited enhanced ECL emission in comparison to pure AgBr NCs which could be attributed to the ability of Ti3C2 as stabilized agents to suppress agglomeration of AgBr NCs and reduce the passivation of AgBr (Jiang et al., 2022b). In 2022, Zhang et al. synthesized a heterostructure of Au NCs@Ti3C2 in situ, utilizing the unique reducibility of Ti3C2, to serve as a luminophore. Au NCs were anchored on the surface of Ti3C2 through O atoms, and Au-O-Ti acted as the connection between Au NCs and Ti3C2. Through combination with catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) and the lateral cutting characteristics of CRISPR-Cas12a, they constructed an ECL biosensor to detect miRNA-155 (Zhang et al., 2022b).

MXene/MXene-based nanocomposites have the potential to go beyond their conventional application. Recent research suggests these nanostructures have the potential for a significantly broader range of applications, extending beyond their well-established role as a platform for immobilizing luminophore molecules and biorecognition elements.

ECL immunoassays typically employ the “on-electrode” approach, specifically utilizing the sandwich-type immunoassay format. Researchers have recently introduced an innovative design of an “in-electrode” Faraday-cage type concept-based immunocomplex immobilization strategy to attain a boosted ECL signal (Kannan et al., 2019). The primary distinction from the conventional sandwich-type approach lies in the “in-electrode” Faraday-cage-type immunoassay's design, which incorporates a conductive two-dimensional (2-D) nanomaterial as the immobilization substrate of the signal unit. In this process, the entire immunoassay system operates similarly to a Faraday cage, taking into account the skin effect of the electric field. The immunocomplex is fully enclosed by a structure akin to a Faraday cage, encompassing all electrochemiluminophores. This configuration allows for unrestricted electron flow between the electrode and the 2-D nanomaterials due to the equipotential nature of the analogous Faraday cage. This strategically extends the outer Helmholtz plane (OHP) of the electrode surface. This positioning effectively confines all the labeled electrochemiluminophores within the confined space between the extended OHP and the electrode itself. As a result, all the luminophores actively participate in the electrochemical reaction at the electrode surface, leading to the generation of strong ECL signals (Guo et al., 2016).

MXene's impressive electrical conductivity, flexibility, and expansive specific surface area have led to its utilization as a proficient “Faraday cage” in development of ECL biosensors. For example, Chen et al. developed an ECL immunosensor to realize the quantitative detection of genetically modified maize MON810 containing Cry1Ab components. They employed Fe3O4 nanoparticles functionalized with capture antibodies (capture unit) and Ti3C2Tx for signal amplification (signal unit) (Fig. 2 A). The signal unit further was incorporated with PEI and 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA) as ECL labels and recognition antibodies. Specific recognition between Cry1Ab and detection antibodies mediates the assembly of the signal unit onto the electrode surface, forming a crucial “capture unit-Cry1Ab-signal unit” complex. This complex signifies the successful construction of the Faraday cage-type immunosensor. The micron-sized, conductive MXene in the signal unit directly attaches to the electrode, extending the OHP and essentially functioning as an extended electrode surface. Confined within the extended OHP due to the Faraday cage effect, PTCA labels in the signal unit behave as if directly electrode-bound. This enables unimpeded electron transfer between PTCA and the electrode leading to a significant enhancement of ECL intensity (Chen et al., 2021b). In another research, Wei et al. developed a dual-mode ECL/SERS immunoassay for the determination of the pathogenic bacteria Vibrio vulnificus. Notably, the Faraday cage-type sensor design ensured the electrochemical activation of all ECL signal tags. Furthermore, Using MXene as the substrate of the signal unit to construct a dual-mode immunosensor, they were able to eliminate the negative impact exerted by the micron-sized dimensions of pathogenic bacteria on signal generation (Wei et al., 2021). Faraday cage-type sensors excel at detecting large analytes like exosomes, vesicles, etc. Recognizing this advantage, researchers have increasingly focused on designing diverse Faraday cage-based ECL sensors in recent years. Faraday cage-type sensors excel at detecting large analytes like exosomes, vesicles, etc. Exosomes' large size hinders electron tunneling at the electrochemical interface due to their positioning beyond the outer Helmholtz plane resulting in a much lower probability of electron tunneling. However, the fabrication of a micro-Faraday cage on the electrode surface offers a promising solution to the issue of low electron tunneling efficiency. By expanding the outer Helmholtz plane, these micro-cages facilitate a significant acceleration of charge transfer, thereby enhancing detection sensitivity. Recognizing this advantage, researchers have increasingly focused on designing diverse Faraday cage-based ECL sensors in recent years (Du et al., 2014). For Example, Nie et al. have recently developed a Ti3C2Tx−bismuth sulfide nanorolls (Bi2S3 NRs)−x heterostructure/engineered lipid layer based nanoarchitecture for detecting gastric cancer exosomes in the ascites (Fig. 2B). To achieve this, they initially modified the electrode surface by Au NP@SiO2. Subsequently, they linked the cholesterol-modified CD63 aptamer to lipid bilayers, enabling the specific recognition of exosomes. Upon immersion of the engineered lipid layer into the patient's ascites, CD63 positive exosomes were recognized and captured from the matrix directly. Meanwhile, active Bi2S3 NRs were synthesized on Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets. Within the Ti3C2Tx−Bi2S3−x heterostructure, sulfur vacancies played a role as effective electron traps, promoting electron injection and enhancing ECL intensity. The exceptional conductivity and high specific surface area of the Ti3C2Tx-Bi2S3-x heterostructure make it an ideal platform for MUC1 aptamer conjugation. This composite effectively detects and captures the fusion bodies of gastric cancer exosomes and the engineered lipid layer. Within the self-luminous Faraday cage-type sensing system, the Ti3C2Tx-Bi2S3-x heterostructure, featuring engineered sulfur vacancies, extends the OHP and amplifies the ECL signal (Nie et al., 2023).

In 2022, Nie et al. expanded the sensing applications of MXenes and introduced a novel approach to comprehend the intrinsic optoelectronic characteristics of MXenes. They synthesized a heterostructure of Ti3C2 as a novel emitter. Although MXene exhibits ECL performance in the presence of strong oxidizing agents like potassium persulfate, the significant background interference caused by potassium persulfate restricts its application as a luminophore in ECL research. The luminescence signal of the MXene material with hydrogen peroxide is likewise exceedingly faint. In their work, they synthesized a gold single-atom confined MXene (AuSA/MXene) heterostructure as a novel ECL functional material with hydrogen peroxide. The confined gold atoms in MXene heterostructure support were highly delocalized due to the electronic metal−support interaction, resulting in a lower energy barrier for the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide into hydroxyl radicals. They further incorporated TiO2 onto the heterostructure to promote the generation of free radicals and transport electrons for MXene. Finally, they used AuSA/MXene heterostructure as a Faraday cage-type ECL emitter with fluid nanoislands to detect miRNA-187 in triple-negative breast cancer tumor tissues (Nie et al., 2022).

Several endeavors have been undertaken to boost the sensitivity of ECL assays, encompassing the quest for a highly luminescent luminophore, investigation of appropriate co-reactants, acceleration of electron transfer reactions, and implementation of target recycling amplification strategies. In 2015, Yuan et al. introduced the concept of “co-reaction accelerator” (Ma et al., 2015b). This concept involves a chemical species that reacts with the co-reactant, rather than the

Luminophores, to expedite the decomposition of the co-reactant and produce more active intermediates (Zhu et al., 2017). The co-reaction accelerator plays a crucial role in significantly boosting the ECL signal. The co-reaction accelerator protocol stands out when compared to other ECL improvement techniques because of its varied, straightforward, and highly effective features.

So far, a variety of substances including inorganic compounds, organic compounds, and nanomaterials have been engineered to serve as co-reaction accelerators. It has been observed that the ECL performance of nanomaterials-based co-reaction accelerators is significantly impacted by factors such as their sizes, shapes, and chemical compositions (Hu et al., 2020; Wang, 2021). This complexity makes it challenging to uncover the correlations between the structure and performance of ECL co-reaction accelerators. Hence, it's highly desirable to design the co-reaction accelerators with well-defined active centers, facilitating a deeper understanding of the fundamental mechanism (Hu et al., 2020). The literature survey unveiled that due to their distinct sizes, shapes, and chemical composition, MXenes-based nanocomposites can act not only as effective carriers for biorecognition elements and luminophores but also showcase remarkable performance as co-reactant accelerators.

Achieving an outstanding ECL performance within the pure Ru (bpy)3 2+/TPrA system presents challenges due to potential issues associated with this system, including high background ECL signal caused by a high TPrA concentration and the complexities involved in immobilizing Ru (bpy)3 2+ onto the electrode surface (Xing et al., 2018). In this context, some special MXene-based nanocomposites have been engineered as potent co-reactant accelerators, aimed at augmenting the ECL efficiency of Ru (bpy)3 2+/TPrA systems.

MXene, due to its surface chemistry characterized by functional groups like hydroxyl (-OH) and oxygen-containing moieties, offers active sites that catalyze the reduction and oxidation of species participating in ECL reactions (Chen et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022b). Some studies have demonstrated the efficacy of transition metal compounds like metal nanoparticles, metal oxides (such as TiO2), and metal carbides as co-reaction accelerators in the electro-oxidation of TPrA to promote the production of reductive TPrA• radicals for signal amplification (Zhang et al., 2019b). TiO2 materials could potentially be synthesized utilizing titanium atoms situated on Ti3C2Tx as nucleation sites. Several reports have investigated the feasibility of utilizing Ti3C2Tx MXenes as a titanium source for fabricating 2D TiO2 nanosheets on the interlayers of Ti3C2Tx MXenes. For example, Yang et al., in 2021, an ECL immunosensing system established using detection antibody (Ab2)-labeled [Ir (ppy)2 (acac)]@PEGylated apoferritin (Ir@PEG-apoHSF) as the signal indicator and capture antibody-incubated Ti3C2Tx/TiO2 hybrids as the sensing substrate. They investigated the application of Ir@PEG-apoHSF as a bioprobe targeting neuron-specific enolase (NSE) with a signal on mechanism. The findings demonstrated that PEGylated apoHSF effectively hosts and stabilizes water-insoluble iridium complexes, serving as ECL emitters for applications in aqueous biosensing and immunoassays. Additionally, their finding illustrated that Ti3C2Tx/TiO2 hybrids possess the ability to catalyze the oxidation of both TPrA and Ir-(ppy)2 (acac) at the electrode interface, thereby enhancing the rate of the ECL reaction, leading to a higher excited state of [Ir (ppy)2 (acac)]* and consequently generating a more intense ECL output (Yang et al., 2021). Recently, Wang et al. have developed a self-enhanced ECL strategy for the detection of MDA-MB-231 exosomes using a porphyrin-based MOF (Por-MOF) (Fig. 3 A). They synthesized Zn-TCPP-TiO2-Ti3C2 by integrating the luminophore (Zn-TCPP) and the in situ generated co-reaction accelerator (TiO2 nanoparticles), using a solvothermal method. The TiO2 nanoparticles were generated in situ based on Ti3C2 MXenes as the nano platform by exploiting the abundant exposed metal sites of Ti3C2 MXenes to provide Ti sources. Subsequently, they immobilized TPrA@AuNPs-Apt 2 probes on the electrode surface to capture exosomes through a sandwich structure. Their findings suggest that the presence of Ti3C2 MXenes in the luminophore and AuNPs in the co-reactant can significantly enhance electron transfer efficiency (Wang et al., 2023c).

Besides MXene based TiO2 nanocomposite, emerging semiconductors like ultrathin bismuth oxychloride doped with a heteroatom like phosphorus (P-BiOCl), as a ternary compound semiconductor, have also attracted interest in this regard. By employing MXene as a scaffold to anchor P–BiOCl nanosheets and facilitate their attachment onto MXene, it becomes feasible to achieve an amplified intensity of the ECL signal (Nasrin et al., 2022; Xia et al., 2018). In a study carried out by Liu in 2022, an ECL immunosensor was developed for the detection of the SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein (Fig. 3B). Firstly, they synthesized a 2D/2D MXene nanocomposite based on sequential anchoring of ultrathin phosphorus-doped bismuth oxychloride (P–BiOCl) and Ru (bpy)3 2+ onto Ti3C2Tx via a solvothermal method and electrostatic interaction. Subsequently, they applied the prepared Ti3C2Tx/P–BiOCl/Ru (bpy)3 2+ hybrids to the electrode surface for the immobilization of Anti-2019-nCoV-N McAb and CoVNP. Ultimately, by binding the target CoVNP to the mentioned heterojunction composite, they measured the decreased ECL intensity. They stated that the amalgamation of bulk and interfacial characteristics within the 2D/2D Ti3C2Tx/P–BiOCl/Ru (bpy)3 2+ composite renders it proficient as a signal enhancer and co-reaction expediter (Liu et al., 2022).

It is well known that K2S2O8, an efficient cathodic co-reactant can significantly improve ECL signals by producing ECL reaction intermediate SO4 •-. However, the restricted solubility of K2S2O8 and the instability of the generated SO4 •- in aqueous solutions lead to a reduced concentration of SO4 •-. Consequently, there is a strong interest in investigating ECL reagents and co-reaction accelerators that offer superior luminescence stability and effective signal enhancement strategies. Some studies suggest that Ti3C2 MXene can exhibit superior co-reactant accelerator performance in inducing the dissociation of S2O8 2− into SO4 •-. In 2020, Shi's group developed an ECL immunosensor for cardiac troponin I (cTn-I) detection, utilizing amino-functionalized Ti3C2 nanosheets as a matrix to capture primary anti-cTn-I antibodies and carbon nitride nanosheets modified with amino-functional MOF as a signal probe. They benefited Ti3C2 nanosheet not only as the matrix to capture primary anti-cTn-I but also as a co-reaction accelerator and generated plenty of SO4 •- (Jiang et al., 2020). In 2023, Liu et al. employed Ti3C2 MXene-Au NPs as an efficient co-reactant accelerator to enhance the intensity and stability of the ECL signal of CdS QDs (Fig. 3C). They developed a paperclip probe (H1+H2) featuring a double-quenching function, coupled with target cycle series cleavage, to establish an ECL biosensing platform for the detection of the antibiotic resistance genes (mecA). As shown in Fig. 3C, Ti2+ in Ti3C2 MXene reduced HAuCl4 and then mixed with CdS QDs synthesized by a highly efficient hydrothermal method to obtain the CdS/Ti3C2-Au nanocomposite with superior ECL performance. Utilizing the negative charge of the paperclip nucleic acid probe along with its modification group enabled the achievement of double-quenching of the ECL signal. This approach, when combined with target cyclic amplification, enhanced the identification of the mecA gene (Liu et al., 2023). In another research conducted by Wei et al. a NIR ECL-RET aptasensor was constructed for the determination of tetracycline. Through a one-pot synthesis, they synthesized Ti3C2 MXene nanocomposites decorated with SnS2 quantum dots (acting as the energy donor) due to Ti3C2's ability to both load the QDs and catalyze S2O8 2⁻ dissociation. The nanocomposites displayed strong ECL emission due to the surface-defect effect induced by the oxygen-containing functional groups on MXene. Then, they employed nonmetallic plasmonic hydrated defective tungsten oxide nanosheets (dWO3•H2O) as energy acceptors due to their robust surface plasmon resonance effect. Finally, they served tetracycline aptamer and its complementary chain as a bridge to connect the energy donor and acceptor (Wei et al., 2023a).

Apart from examining the correction accelerator impact of Ti3C2 and Ti3C2-based nanocomposites, some investigations have delved into the potential of less prevalent MXene-based nanocomposites to exhibit similar effects. For example, in 2020 Fang et al. constructed an ECL immunosensor for thyroglobulin detection (Fig. 3D). They synthesized TiO2 nanodots functionalized with ε-polylysine (TiO2 NDs@ε-PL) to serve as a sensing platform for immobilizing the primary antibody. Following the preparation of Nb2CTx to adsorb Ru (bpy)3 2+, they immobilized ZnO quantum dots and secondary antibody respectively, on Nb2CTx and constructed Nb2CTx@Ru (bpy)3 2+- ZnO QDs with a high correction accelerator efficiency in the Ru (bpy)3 2+/S2O8 2− system (Fang et al., 2020a).

The conventional Luminol-O2 and Luminol-H2O2 systems have been extensively employed because of their low toxicity. Nevertheless, these systems encounter issues like the self-decomposition of H2O2 co-reactants, limited content and instability of dissolved oxygen, as well as low reaction efficiency of dissolved oxygen which lead to the restricted conversion efficiency of H2O2 and O2 into reactive oxygen species (ROS) including peroxides, hydroxyl radicals (HO•), and superoxide radicals (O2 •−).

Recent studies have highlighted the promise of Ti3C2-based nanocomposites, particularly Ti3C2-TiO2 for effectively promoting the generation of ROS. Therefore, Ti3C2-TiO2 stands out not only for its remarkable capacity to accelerate the transformation of TPrA to TPrA• but also for its exceptional ability to catalyze the conversion of H2O2 and O2 into ROS by strategically exploiting its inherent oxygen vacancies (Ma et al., 2020). Sun et al. recently synthesized Ti3C2-TiO2-AuNPs nanoprobes through the in-situ synthesis of Au nanoparticles and TiO2 on Ti3C2 nanosheets to the determination of exosomal CD63. They employed this nanohybrid in tandem with high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) pretreatment as an environmentally friendly strategy, utilizing the cavitation effect to produce O2 −• and H2O2 in situ, acting as an initiator. According to their finding, Ti3C2-TiO2-AuNPs nanoprobes not only catalyze H2O2 but also facilitate the aggregation of ROS produced in situ through ultrasound pretreatment. This process shortens the diffusion distance and enhances the rate of intermediate utilization, consequently improving the ECL emission for air-saturated luminol (Zhang et al., 2023a). Inspired by this strategy, Wang's group employed HIFU pretreatment combined with Ti3C2−TiO2 to construct a luminol−O2 ECL system for the detection of polynucleotide kinase. Initially, HIFU triggered the in-situ generation of ROS through cavitation, acting as a co-reactant to amplify luminol emission. Subsequently, Ti3C2−TiO2 was synthesized in situ, capable of aggregating and catalyzing ROS formed in situ by HIFU. Ultimately, the interaction between the Ti present on the Ti3C2−TiO2 surface and phosphate groups via chelation enabled the detection of PNK (Wei et al., 2023b).

In addition to the Ti3C2-TiO2 nanocomposite, other semiconductor materials with notable catalytic activity, favorable biocompatibility, chemical stability, and outstanding photocatalytic and electrocatalytic activities have been utilized in the fabrication of Ti3C2-based nanocomposites, showcasing a high ability for accelerating the generation of ROS. CoS2 and MoS2 are known for their advantageous electrocatalytic activity, customizable nanostructures, and impressive 3D electron configuration. Despite their remarkable potential in heterogeneous catalysis, they require a suitable support material to reach their full electrocatalytic potential (Tran et al., 2018). For instance, in 2023, Zhang et al. explored the co-reaction acceleration efficiency of S-Ti3C2Tx@CoS2. They coupled the micro-nano bubble strategy (aimed at increasing oxygen content) with S-Ti3C2Tx@CoS2 heterostructure for chloramphenicol determination. They observed that the integration of CoS2 nanowires with S-Ti3C2 nanosheets resulted in improved electrocatalytic performance compared to pure CoS2, leading to enhanced efficiency in reducing O2 and generating more ROS. Furthermore, they noted that incorporating sulfur atoms into Ti3C2 could be regarded as a significant approach to enhance the electrochemical performance of Ti3C2 nanosheets. When compared to undoped Ti3C2, sulfur doping can elevate electrical conductivity and improve interlayer spacing, facilitating rapid electron transfer and enhancing overall electrochemical reactivity (Zhang et al., 2023b). In a new study, Ren et al. have achieved to enhanced ECL signal of luminol@ Au@Ni−Co nanocages by employing Ti3C2Tx@TiO2−MoS2 hybrids as co-reaction accelerators for the detection of CYFRA 21−1. They synthesized Ti3C2Tx@TiO2−MoS2 hybrids by initiating the growth of TiO2 nanosheets on Ti3C2Tx and then uniformly growing MoS2 on Ti3C2Tx@TiO2 surfaces using the hydrothermal method. The abundance of active sites on the Ti3C2Tx surface facilitated the loading of a substantial quantity of TiO2 nanosheets and MoS2 nanospheres, resulting in a significantly enhanced dispersion of the active components (Ren et al., 2024).

A particularly captivating application of the peroxidase-like activity within MXene-based nanocomposites emerges in glucose detection. Leveraging MXene's capacity to maintain a high loading of glucose oxidase and peroxidase-like activity for generating ROS from in situ-produced H2O2 via a glucose oxidase (GOx) catalyzed process paves the way for pioneering highly sensitive MXene based ECL in the realm of enzymatic glucose biosensors. For example, Zarei et al. utilized bromophenol blue-delaminated multilayer Ti3C2 MXene as a co-reaction accelerator to enhance the ECL reaction rate of luminol and the co-reactant H2O2 (Fig. 3E). Additionally, they employed it as a substrate to retain a high loading of GOx-conjugated PEI along with luminophore species within the more open structure of Ti3C2 MXene for glucose detection. In the presence of glucose, in situ generating H2O2 product through a GOx catalyzed process could produce abundant OH• radicals via the peroxidase-like activity of the BPB@Ti3C2 in the luminol ECL reaction (Momeni et al., 2024).

Nanomaterial-derived quantum dots (QDs) are exceptional ECL luminophores and hold a significant role in optical sensing (Zhang et al., 2022a). They are synthesized using nanomaterials as precursors or templates, which serve as the building blocks for the quantum dots and provide a platform for controlled growth and manipulation of their size, shape, and composition. In this context, the emergence of MXene-derived quantum dots (MXQDs) has sparked significant interest for a broad spectrum of applications (Cheng et al., 2023; Shao et al., 2020).

When reducing the size of MXene blocks to the 10 nm scale, they transform into MXQDs, which exhibit several distinctive physical and chemical properties while still retaining some of the inherent advantages of the parent MXene block's two-dimensional structure (Jin et al., 2020). Similar to MXene blocks, MXQDs present a significant specific surface area; however, compared to MXene blocks, they offer enhanced biocompatibility, greater tunability of properties, improved compatibility with other nanomaterials for hybridization, and simplified doping or functionalization processes (Abdelsalam and Zhang, 2022; Iravani and Varma, 2022). Furthermore, owing to their quantum confinement and edge effects, MXQDs showcase distinctive electronic and optical characteristics that surpass those of MXene nanosheets and can readily segregate into electrons and holes due to their elevated dielectric constant and extinction coefficient. They find utility across various applications, including sensing, biomedicine, catalysis, optoelectronic devices, energy storage, and conversion, among others, owing to their distinct properties compared to their 2D-MXene counterparts (Deng et al., 2023a). Several studies have suggested that MXQDs hold promise for use in ECL sensing, serving as a prospective ECL emitter. For example, in 2022.

Ma et al. constructed an ECL biosensor for the detection of miRNA221 based on glutathione-capped MXQDs (GSH-MQDs) and magnetic biomimic vesicles (Fig. 4 A). They employed a cholesterol-modified hairpin DNA capable of freely moving on the vesicles as a recognition element for the target miRNA. After the hairpin DNA captured the target miRNA221 during the ECL sensing process, the biomimetic magnetic vesicles were split and drawn straight toward the magnetic electrode. Subsequently, T7 exonuclease was applied to cleave the double-stranded structure and liberate miRNA221. Upon binding the hairpin DNA with the GSHMQD-labeled probe-DNA, the ECL signal of GSH-MXQDs was produced, showing a positive correlation with the concentration of target miRNA221. Therefore, by incorporating a combination of metal atoms from MXene and the sulfhydryl group of GSH into the nanostructures of MXQDs, they succeed in the synthesis of MXQDs while preserving the advantages of MXene (Li et al., 2022). In 2022, Jiang's team synthesized nitrogen-doped titanium carbide QDs (N-Ti3C2 QDs) through a straightforward hydrothermal process using Ti3C2 as the precursor and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen for determination of mucin 1 (MUC1). Initially, they modified the electrode surface with Au nanoparticles to immobilize capture MUC1 antibody. Subsequently, they developed the ECL signal probe by employing AgPt nanospheres to immobilize N-Ti3C2 QDs, enabling the detection of MUC1 antibodies through the Ag-N and Pt-N bond. They found that N-Ti3C2 QDs not only demonstrated high ECL properties and quantum efficiency but also catalyzed the reduction of co-reactant S2O8 2− to generate abundant sulfate radicals (Jiang et al., 2022c). In another research conducted by Nie et al. an ECL sensing platform was developed composed of MXQDs@gold nanobones (Au NBs) heterostructure to determine miRNA-26a. They synthesized MXQDs@Au NBs via the green preparation process, which avoided the harm of hydrofluoric acid to humans and the environment. They observed a strong ECL signal in the MXQDs@Au NBs heterostructure. Gold nanobeads (Au NBs) possessing surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effects have the capability to regulate the over-injection of electrons into the conduction band of MXQDs by transferring electrons to themselves. This mechanism effectively suppresses ECL quenching resulting from short-distance electron transfers between the luminophore and Au nanomaterial (Nie et al., 2021).

The majority of research on MXQDs primarily relies on Ti3C2, which represent the most classical member of the MXene family. However, there are also studies exploring the optical and electrical properties of Nb2C MQDs, Ti3CN QDs and bimetallic Mo2TiC2 QDs. In 2023 Ma et al. developed an ECL biosensor based on bimetallic Mo2TiC2 QDs and SnS2 nanosheets/lipid bilayer to detect the gastric cancer marker miRNA-27a-3p. They synthesized Mo2TiC2 QDs via the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method (Fig. 4B). To immobilize capture DNA modified by cholesterol, they employed SnS2 nanosheets coated with phospholipid bilayer as sensing interfaces (H1). When miRNA-27a-3p was captured, the target-catalyzed hairpin assembly (CHA) cycle was triggered. During the cycling process, probe DNA (H2) labeled Mo2TiC2 QDs can be linked to the sensing interface. As a result, the ECL signal of Mo2TiC2 QDs was positively correlated with the concentration of miRNA-27a-3p. They found that the inter-band excitation of Mo2TiC2 QDs could introduce additional carriers, which were less affected by boundary effects and made a substantial contribution to the luminescence process. Additionally, the semiconductor Mo2TiC2 help inhibit the formation of an internal electric field and potential oxidation. Consequently, Mo2TiC2 QDs exhibited superior luminous intensity and enhanced stability (Li et al., 2023b). In another research, they developed a surface plasmon-coupled ECL (SPC-ECL) biosensor based on bismuth nano-nest and Ti3CN QDs to detect miRNA-421 in gastric cancer ascites. According to Fig. 4C, they produced Ti3CN quantum dots through etching of the Ti3AlCN MAX phase and microwave-assisted intercalation. In the structure of Ti3CN, nitrogen replaced half of the carbon. Additionally, they employed the bismuth nano-nest structure, modified with a phospholipid layer, as an ECL sensing interface to capture DNA. According to their findings, the introduction of electronegative nitrogen led to enhanced physical and electronic properties of Ti3CN. Nitrogen doping in Ti3CN QDs enhanced both luminescence performance and catalytic activity. The plentiful surface plasmon hot spots created among the bismuth nano-nests also contributed to enhancing the isotropic ECL signal of Ti3CN QDs (Li et al., 2023e). Recently, Dai's group proposed a burgeoning diversified closed bipolar ECL (d-BPE-ECL) based on a photothermal amplification biosensor using the thermophysical and photochemical capabilities of Nb2C MXQDs for determination of hepatitis C virus envelope protein E2. They devised a recognition compartment that functions as a temperature-sensitive, conductivity-modulated interface. To enhance its performance, they introduced a photothermal material, specifically PDA@Nb2C MXQDs, using a target-mediated rolling circle amplification strategy. This addition effectively raises the interface temperature when exposed to near-infrared light radiation. As a result, the BPE current is boosted, leading to the amplification of the anode ECL signal from the Nb2C MXQDs (Gao et al., 2024).

Typically, MOFs are formed through coordination reactions occurring between soluble inorganic metal salts and organic ligands in polar solvents. To achieve well-defined morphologies in MOFs, some different strategies employing insoluble metal precursors have been explored (Liu et al., 2012). Due to its high surface area, MXene provides numerous active sites that precisely control the nucleation process, guiding the formation of mesoscopic architectures. It therefore serves as an ideal precursor for this purpose (Wu et al., 2019). The terminal atoms located on the MXene surface possess low work function and high electronegativity. This characteristic enables them to act as strong electron acceptors, facilitating the deprotonation of organic ligands and their subsequent bridging with the underlying metal atoms (Hu et al., 2017). Meanwhile, the extensive accessible surfaces of the atomically thin 2D MXene provide abundant attack sites for the protonated ligands, accelerating the reaction process while preserving the underlying 2D topology (Wu et al., 2019).

Because of the extensive fluidity and stability of electrons within the ring, porphyrin compounds exhibit remarkable luminescent, electrochemical, and photoelectric characteristics. They, featuring a substantial planar aromatic ring and protonated carboxylic groups, can readily interact with and bind to the surface atoms of MXene (Gan et al., 2021; Han et al., 2020). Recent research has explored the fabrication of porphyrin-based MOFs (P-MOFs) using MXene as the metal precursor. These studies demonstrate that aggregating porphyrin compounds into MOF structures can enhance their ECL intensity. For example, in 2022, Wei et al. synthesized a 2D MOF sheet using Ti3C2Tx MXene as the metal precursor and meso-tetra(4-carboxyl-phenyl) porphyrin (H2TCPP) as the organic ligand. The deprotonation of H2TCPP was aided by the terminal atoms in Ti3C2Tx MXene's high electronegativity and low work function. Thereafter, the 2D MOF sheet was formed by the deprotonated H2TCPP bonding with Ti atoms.They also embedded Ag NPs into the Ti3C2Tx-PMOF sheet, which inherited the ECL activity from H2TCPP, to increase the ECL intensity. In order to further increase biosensor sensitivity, they used a bipedal three-dimensional DNA walker strategy, which allowed them to successfully detect oral cancer overexpressed 1 (ORAOV1) (Wei et al., 2022). In addition to Ti3C2Tx-based P-MOFs, researchers have also successfully synthesized p-MOF using other types of MXene as metal precursors. In a recent study conducted by Li et al. a dual-photoelectrochemical (PEC) and ECL aptasensor was established for the detection of miRNA-126 using V2CTx MXene-derived porphyrin-based MOF embedded with Ag NPs. Because of the existence of V nodes in V2CTx MXene nanosheets, they synthesized a V-based MOF using tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin as a ligand, and subsequently introduced Ag+ ions to create the AgNPs@V-MOF Schottky heterojunction. Taking advantage of the rapid electron transfer facilitated by the V2CTx substrate and the harmonized band-edge energy levels between the light-sensitive Ag NPs and V-PMOF, they established the AgNPs@V-MOF Schottky heterojunction. This structure demonstrated enhanced transfer of the photogenerated carriers, resulting in superior performances in both PEC and ECL assays (Li et al., 2023d).

Since the unveiling of MXenes in 2011, significant endeavors have been directed towards their synthesis, surface modification, and utilization across various domains. So far numerous strategies for synthesis and surface functionalization/modification have been explored, offering insights into their scalability and applicability across diverse fields of science and technology. Recent progress in MXene synthesis, functionalization techniques, exploitation of their distinctive properties, and the development of innovative composites have significantly enhanced their potential in ECL applications. The burgeoning field of ECL sensor development has witnessed the strategic exploitation of MXenes to achieve significant amplification of the ECL signal. This review offered an extensive overview of all the diverse strategies and approaches currently employed in the fabrication of MXene-based ECL sensors and biosensors. We thoroughly explored the exceptional properties of MXenes/MXene-based nanocomposites, emphasizing the characteristics that make them particularly well-suited for ECL sensing and biosensing applications.

First and foremost, we explored the exceptional potential of MXenes/MXene-based nanocomposites as supports for the immobilization of biorecognition elements and luminophores, which represent a prominent application area for these materials. Next, we presented the Faraday cage-type ECL biosensing platform to demonstrate that these nanostructures can serve not only as a substrate (capture unit) but also as a signal unit. To further illustrate the extensive applicability of MXenes/MXene-based nanocomposites, we subsequently explored the concept of co-reactant accelerators. This exploration focused on the ability of these nanostructures to enhance the ECL signal, thereby expanding their potential applications in this field. Finally, we investigated the application of MXenes/MXene-based nanocomposites as precursors for the synthesis of novel ECL emitters. By comprehensively exploring the role of MXenes/MXene-based nanocomposites and meticulously dissecting the spectrum of fabrication strategies, this review empowers readers to grasp the precise advantages these materials offer in the development of ECL sensors and biosensors.

Despite the promising potential of MXenes for sensing applications, several key challenges must be addressed before widespread adoption. The primary concern lies in the environmental impact associated with their current synthesis process. Mass production of MXenes often relies on strong HF etchants, which pose significant environmental risks. Furthermore, precisely controlling the surface functionalization of MXene remains a significant technical challenge. For example, MXenes functionalized with the –F group may not be suitable for biosensing in certain scenarios, whereas those functionalized with the –OH or –O group are anticipated to offer improved sensing capabilities. Detailed investigations into the long-term toxicity of MXenes are also warranted.

While MXene/MXene-based nanocomposites have already found wide application in the development of ECL sensors and biosensors (Table 1), the future holds even greater promise for their use. Several exciting avenues remain open for further exploration in this area. A critical area for future exploration lies in enhancing the stability and biocompatibility of MXenes. This may be achieved through targeted surface modifications and the development of novel composite materials. Moreover, miniaturizing and integrating MXene-based ECL biosensors into portable and wearable devices hold immense promise for revolutionizing point-of-care diagnostics. This technological advancement could lead to real-time, on-site detection of biomarkers with high sensitivity and specificity. The ongoing development of advanced fabrication techniques, including 3D printing and flexible electronics, is poised to further contribute to the creation of next-generation biosensors with enhanced performance and versatility. In conclusion, sustained interdisciplinary research and collaboration across the fields of nanotechnology, materials science, and biomedical engineering will be paramount to unlocking the full potential of MXene-based nanocomposites for groundbreaking and impactful biosensing applications.

Nastaran Arab: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Morteza Hosseini: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Guobao Xu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.
